section.title: Rejection and the Death of John
section.sectionRationale: The tone darkens. Jesus is rejected in His own hometown, marvelling at their unbelief. He sends the Twelve out in vulnerable dependence to preach repentance. Then Mark tells, in grim flashback, how Herod murdered John the Baptist, the forerunner whose fate foreshadows the fate of the One he announced. Rejection of God's messengers is the shadow over the mission.
section.thesis: The kingdom advances through rejected and vulnerable messengers: Jesus dishonoured at home, the Twelve sent out with nothing but His authority, and John the Baptist put to death by a weak king's pride, so that the servants share their Master's path of suffering even as the word goes forward.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the rejection at Nazareth; the sending of the Twelve; and the martyrdom of John the Baptist at Herod's banquet.

units.label: The rejection at Nazareth
units.note: a prophet without honor at home

verses.text: Jesus went on from there and came to His hometown, accompanied by His disciples.

When the Sabbath came, He began to teach in the synagogue, and many who heard Him were astonished. “Where did this man get these ideas?” they asked. “What is this wisdom He has been given? And how can He perform such miracles?
Isn’t this the carpenter, the son of Mary and the brother of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon? Aren’t His sisters here with us as well?” And they took {{skandalizo|offense}} at Him.
Then Jesus said to them, “Only in his hometown, among his relatives, and in his own household is a {{prophetes|prophet}} without honor.”
So He could not perform any miracles there, except to lay His hands on a few of the sick and heal them.
And He was amazed at their {{apistia|unbelief}}. And He went around from village to village, teaching the people.
Then Jesus called the Twelve to Him and began to send them out two by two, giving them authority over unclean spirits.
He instructed them to take nothing but a staff for the journey, no bread, no bag, no money in their belts,
and to wear sandals, but not a second tunic.
And He told them, “When you enter a house, stay there until you leave that area.
If anyone will not welcome you or listen to you, shake the dust off your feet when you leave that place, as a testimony against them.”
So they set out and preached that the people should {{metanoeo|repent}}.
They also drove out many demons and healed many of the sick, anointing them with oil.

units.label: The martyrdom of John the Baptist

verses.text: Now King Herod heard about this, for Jesus’ name had become well known, and people were saying, “John the Baptist has risen from the dead! That is why miraculous powers are at work in him.”
Others were saying, “He is Elijah,” and still others, “He is a prophet, like one of the prophets of old.”
But when Herod heard this, he said, “John, whom I beheaded, has risen from the dead!”
For Herod himself had ordered that John be arrested and bound and imprisoned, on account of his brother Philip’s wife Herodias, whom Herod had married.
For John had been telling Herod, “It is not lawful for you to have your brother’s wife!”
So Herodias held a grudge against John and wanted to kill him. But she had been unable,
because Herod feared John and protected him, knowing that he was a {{dikaios|righteous}} and holy man. When he heard John’s words, he was greatly perplexed; yet he listened to him gladly.
On Herod’s birthday, her opportunity arose. Herod held a banquet for his nobles and military commanders and the leading men of Galilee.
When the daughter of Herodias came and danced, she pleased Herod and his guests, and the king said to the girl, “Ask me for whatever you wish, and I will give it to you.”
And he swore to her, “Whatever you ask of me, I will give you, up to half my kingdom!”
Then she went out and asked her mother, “What should I request?” And her mother answered, “The head of John the Baptist.”
At once the girl hurried back to the king with her request: “I want you to give me the head of John the Baptist on a platter immediately.”
The king was consumed with sorrow, but because of his oaths and his guests, he did not want to refuse her.
So without delay, the king commanded that John’s head be brought in. He sent an executioner, who went and beheaded him in the prison.
The man brought John’s head on a platter and presented it to the girl, who gave it to her mother.
When John’s disciples heard about this, they came and took his body and placed it in a tomb.

skandalizo.gloss: To cause to stumble, to give offense, to trip up. The people of Nazareth are tripped up by Jesus' familiar origins; because they think they know Him, they refuse to reckon with the wisdom and power staring them in the face. Familiarity breeds a stumbling unbelief.

prophetes.gloss: A prophet, one who speaks God's word. Jesus places Himself in the line of the prophets who were dishonoured by their own, a line that runs through John the Baptist and reaches back through Israel's history of resisting God's messengers.

apistia.gloss: Unbelief, faithlessness. Jesus marvels at the unbelief of Nazareth, the opposite of the faith He commended in the bleeding woman and Jairus. Unbelief does not defeat His power but does close the door on receiving it, so that He does few mighty works there.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, to change one's mind and turn to God. The Twelve carry the same message Jesus and John preached: repent. The kingdom's advance calls for the turning of the whole life, and the messengers press this summons from town to town.

dikaios.gloss: Righteous, just, upright. Even Herod recognized John as a righteous and holy man and feared him. Yet recognition without repentance could not save Herod from his pride; he silenced the righteous man rather than heed him, a warning about hearing truth gladly yet not obeying it.

basic.context: Jesus returns to Nazareth, and the people who watched Him grow up cannot get past the carpenter they think they know; they take offense, and He marvels at their unbelief. He then sends the Twelve out two by two with almost nothing, dependent on His authority and on the hospitality of others, to preach repentance, cast out demons, and heal. Mark then pauses to tell how John the Baptist died: how the fearless prophet rebuked Herod's unlawful marriage, how Herodias schemed, and how a rash oath at a birthday banquet led to John's beheading. The messenger who prepared the way meets a violent end.
basic.hermeneutics: This section is about the cost of the mission. Jesus is rejected at home, the Twelve are sent in vulnerability and will meet rejection too, and John is martyred. John's death, told after the sending of the Twelve, casts its shadow forward over Jesus and all His servants. The gladly-listening but disobedient Herod is a sober study in hearing without heeding.
basic.originalLanguages: Nazareth is skandalizo, tripped up, by the familiar Jesus, and He marvels at their apistia, unbelief. He is a prophetes, a prophet, without honour at home. The Twelve preach that people should metanoeo, repent. And Herod knew John was dikaios, righteous, yet killed him anyway.
basic.typology: John the Baptist is the new Elijah, and his conflict with Herod and Herodias echoes Elijah's with Ahab and Jezebel. The dishonoured prophet and the murdered forerunner both prefigure the rejection and death of Christ Himself.
basic.christ: Jesus is the prophet dishonoured by His own, walking the road every faithful messenger walks. John's fate is a preview of Jesus' own arrest and death. Even the speculation about who Jesus is, John raised, Elijah, a prophet, circles the deeper truth Mark has already told us.
basic.god: God's word goes forward through weak and rejected messengers, not through worldly power. God calls His servants to depend on Him, and He honours faithfulness even to death.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 19 and 21 set Elijah against Ahab and Jezebel. Matthew 10 parallels the sending of the Twelve. Hebrews 11 honours the prophets who suffered.
basic.oneStory: Israel's long history of rejecting God's prophets reaches into the ministry of Jesus and John. The forerunner is killed, foreshadowing the death of the One he announced. Yet the kingdom does not depend on human welcome; it advances through faithful, vulnerable, even martyred messengers, all the way to the cross where the ultimate rejected Messenger wins the world's salvation.

context.literary: Mark frames the sending of the Twelve, verses 7 to 13, with a sandwich: he sends them out, then fills the interval with the flashback of John's death, verses 14 to 29, before the apostles return in verse 30. The technique links the disciples' mission to the reality of rejection and martyrdom, and John's death anticipates Jesus'. The Nazareth rejection opens the section on a note of unbelief.
context.historical: Nazareth was a small village where Jesus was known as the local carpenter. Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee, had married Herodias, his brother's former wife, which John denounced as unlawful under Leviticus 18 and 20. Rulers held lavish birthday banquets, and rash public oaths before guests created a point of honour a weak ruler feared to break.
context.canonical: John stands as the last and greatest of the prophets and the new Elijah, Malachi 4:5 and Matthew 11:14, and his clash with Herod and Herodias mirrors Elijah against Ahab and Jezebel, 1 Kings 19 and 21. The dishonoured prophet motif runs from the Old Testament, 2 Chronicles 36:16, into the rejection of Jesus and the persecution of the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the kingdom advances through rejected and vulnerable messengers, to instruct the reader in the cost of following and proclaiming Jesus, and, by placing John's martyrdom beside the mission of the Twelve, to foreshadow the suffering that awaits Jesus and His servants.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific travel instructions to the Twelve, no bread, no bag, one tunic, were for that mission in that setting and are not a fixed rule for all ministry; Jesus later adjusts them, Luke 22:35-36. What abides is the principle of dependent, unencumbered service, the call to repentance, and the certainty that faithful witness may cost dearly.

debates.question: Why could Jesus do no mighty work in Nazareth?

views.label: A moral, not a metaphysical, limitation
views.case: It was not that unbelief stripped Jesus of power but that in a context of settled rejection He withheld the mighty works, since miracles were meant to accompany faith, not to overwhelm hardened contempt.

views.label: Miracles fitted to receptive faith
views.case: Jesus still healed a few of the sick; the point is that mighty works answer to faith that seeks Him, and Nazareth's unbelief closed the door on the blessing they might have received.

debates.bearing: Both readings guard against two errors: Jesus is not powerless before unbelief, yet He is not a spectacle-worker who forces His gifts on those who despise Him; unbelief forfeits blessing.

debates.question: What does Herod's gladly listening yet killing John teach?

views.label: The danger of admiring truth without obeying it
views.case: Herod feared John, protected him, and listened gladly, yet would not repent; interest and even conscience are not obedience, and pride finally silenced the very voice he respected.

views.label: The tyranny of pride and human respect
views.case: Herod killed John not from conviction but to save face before his guests over a rash oath; the fear of man and the weakness of a proud heart drove him to murder against his own better sense.

debates.bearing: Both expose the peril of a heart moved by the truth yet mastered by pride and the fear of others, hearing the word gladly while refusing to bow to it.

study.originalLanguages: The people of Nazareth are skandalizo, tripped up, by the very familiarity of Jesus, and He marvels at their apistia, their unbelief, the tragic opposite of the faith that draws out His power. He names Himself a prophetes, a prophet without honour among his own, joining the long line of rejected messengers. The Twelve go out preaching that people should metanoeo, repent, the whole-life turning the kingdom demands. And Herod, who knew John to be dikaios, a righteous man, and feared and even enjoyed him, still handed him to the executioner, a portrait of truth admired but not obeyed.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus rejected by His own, in the line of the prophets and fully in His own rejection and death, John 1:11 and Luke 13:33-34.

typology.type: John as the new Elijah against a wicked court
typology.fulfillment: The forerunner confronting Herod and Herodias as Elijah confronted Ahab and Jezebel, Malachi 4:5 and 1 Kings 21:17-24.
John's death foreshadowing the death of Christ, the greater One whose way he prepared, Mark 9:12-13.

study.christ: Jesus appears here as the prophet without honour in His own hometown, treading the path every faithful messenger of God has walked and that He will walk to its end. The crowd's guesses, that He is John raised, or Elijah, or a prophet of old, hover around a truth far greater than they imagine, the truth Mark told us in his first line. And John's martyrdom is a signpost to the cross: the forerunner who announced Jesus also goes before Him in death at the hands of a fearful, proud ruler. The rejection that gathers around Jesus will crest at Calvary, where the ultimate rejected Messenger accomplishes the world's salvation.
study.god: God advances His purposes not through the machinery of worldly power but through rejected, vulnerable, and even martyred servants. He sends the Twelve out with nothing but His authority and the hospitality of others, teaching that His mission depends on Him, not on resources. He honours the faithfulness of John, who spoke truth to a king and paid with his life. And He warns, through Herod, that hearing the word gladly is not the same as obeying it; God is not mocked by admiration that refuses repentance.

crossReferences.note: Elijah confronting Ahab and Jezebel over their sin, the pattern John follows against Herod and Herodias.
The parallel commissioning and instructions to the Twelve for their mission.
He came to His own, and His own did not receive Him, the rejection begun at Nazareth.
Israel mocked God's messengers and despised His words, the long history of prophet-rejection Jesus and John share.

study.oneStory: Israel's sad history of rejecting the prophets flows straight into the ministry of Jesus and John. The One who prepared the way is beheaded by a weak king's pride, and Jesus Himself is dishonoured in His own town, while the Twelve are sent out to meet welcome and rejection alike. The kingdom does not ride on human approval; it comes through faithful messengers who share their Master's road of suffering. John's death casts its shadow forward to the cross, where the greatest rejected Messenger turns the world's no to God into the means of its salvation.

quiz.q: Why did the people of Nazareth take offense at Jesus?

options.text: Their familiarity with Him as the local carpenter tripped them into unbelief, despite His evident wisdom and miracles.
options.note: Mark 6:3-6; they could not see past His ordinary origins and stumbled into unbelief.

options.text: Because His teaching was too hard to understand.
options.note: They were astonished at His wisdom; the problem was not comprehension but offense at His familiar background.

options.text: Because He performed no miracles among them at all.
options.note: He did heal a few; their unbelief came first and limited the works, rather than the reverse.

options.text: Because He claimed not to be from Nazareth.
options.note: They knew Him as a local; His origins were the very cause of their offense.

quiz.q: How did Jesus send out the Twelve?

options.text: Two by two, with His authority over demons but almost no provisions, dependent on Him and on others' hospitality.
options.note: Mark 6:7-9; they took a staff only, relying on God and hosts.

options.text: Two by two to preach and heal in the towns.
options.note: Correct as far as it goes, but it omits the striking vulnerability and dependence of taking nothing.

options.text: Alone, each to a different distant nation.
options.note: They went two by two in nearby towns, not singly to distant nations.

options.text: With ample supplies and money for a long journey.
options.note: The opposite; He told them to take nothing but a staff.

quiz.q: What message did the Twelve preach?

options.text: That people should repent, the same call Jesus and John proclaimed.
options.note: Mark 6:12; they preached repentance.

options.text: That people should be healed and freed from demons.
options.note: They did heal and cast out demons, but their preached message was the call to repent.

options.text: That the Twelve themselves should be honoured.
options.note: They went in humble dependence, pointing to the kingdom, not to themselves.

options.text: That the law of Moses was now abolished.
options.note: Their message was repentance in view of the kingdom, not the abolition of the law.

quiz.q: Why did Herod have John the Baptist beheaded?

options.text: Pride and human respect: a rash oath before his guests, exploited by Herodias, overrode his own fear and regard for John.
options.note: Mark 6:26-27; Herod acted to save face despite knowing John was righteous.

options.text: Because John had rebuked his unlawful marriage to Herodias.
options.note: That was the underlying grievance, but the immediate cause was Herod's oath and fear of his guests.

options.text: Because John had plotted against Herod's throne.
options.note: John had not plotted; he had spoken the truth about Herod's marriage.

options.text: Because Herod had never respected John.
options.note: Herod feared and protected John and listened to him gladly, which makes the murder more tragic.

quiz.q: What does Herod's example warn us about?

options.text: That one can hear the truth gladly and even admire the messenger, yet refuse to repent and obey.
options.note: Herod listened gladly but would not turn, and pride finally silenced the voice he respected.

options.text: That powerful people often dislike prophets.
options.note: True in general, but Herod actually liked John; the sharper warning is admiring truth without obeying it.

options.text: That making promises is always sinful.
options.note: The issue was a rash, sinful oath kept out of pride, not the making of promises as such.

options.text: That God's messengers are always protected from harm.
options.note: John was martyred; faithful witness can cost one's life.

questions: The people of Nazareth missed Jesus because they thought they already knew Him. Where might overfamiliarity be dulling your wonder at who Jesus is?
The Twelve were sent out in radical dependence on God and others. What would it look like for you to serve God with less self-reliance and more trust?
John spoke the truth to a powerful man even at great cost. Where is God calling you to speak or live the truth when it would be safer to stay silent?
Herod listened to John gladly but would not repent. Is there truth you enjoy hearing but have not yet obeyed?
John's faithfulness led to a martyr's death, yet he prepared the way for the Saviour. How does John's example shape your view of a life measured by faithfulness rather than by comfort or success?

translationNotes.note: The instructions originally used dashes to set off the list of what not to take; rendered here with commas, no bread, no bag, no money in their belts, without changing the sense.
Some manuscripts read he was saying rather than people were saying; either way the report circulated that John had risen, which pricked Herod's guilty conscience.
Some early manuscripts read his daughter Herodias rather than the daughter of Herodias; the girl is the daughter of Herodias in either case, and the account is unaffected.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 6:1-29. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dashes in verse 8 were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
